# One-step solvothermal synthesis of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite as magnetically recyclable mimetic peroxidase



# Zhonghua Wang * , Ming Chen, Jinxia Shu, Yang Li


Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, China West Normal University, Nanchong637002, PR China


# a r t i c l e i n f o


Article history: Received21February2016Received in revised form10April2016Accepted26April2016Available online28April2016


# Keywords:


One-step method Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite Mimetic peroxidase H2O2detection Magnetic recyclability and reusability


# 1. Introduction


Natural enzymes, due to their high efficiency and mild reaction conditions, have been extensively studied and widely used in thefield of medicine, chemical industry, food processing and agriculture [1,2], environmental monitoring and protection [3,4]. However, natural enzymes have their own intrinsic drawbacks and disadvantages, such as degradation by protease, denaturation under strong acidic and alkaline conditions, and at high temperature conditions, as well as the requirement of specials storage conditions [5,6]. Moreover, the preparation, purification and storage of natural enzymes are usually expensive and time-consuming [7,8]. Therefore, the synthesis of artificial enzyme mimics has long been a major concern of chemists and biochemists [9e13].

Recently, Gao et al. has reported that Fe3O4nanoparticles possess intrinsic enzyme activity similar to that of natural peroxidases such as horseradish peroxidase (HRP), which opens the door to the development of nanoscaled inorganic materials in biochemicalfield [14]. Since this report, a number of nanoscaled metal oxides, metals and metal alloys, such as Co3O4-nanorods [15],

# a b s t r a c t


Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite was prepared by a simple and facile one-step solvothermal method and characterized by XRD, SEM, TEM, EDS and XPS techniques. The Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite showed peroxidase-like activity, it could catalyze the oxidation of typical peroxidase substrates, o -phenylenediamine (OPDA) and3,3 0 ,5,5 0 -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). A simple colorimetric method for the detection of H2O2could be established based on the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite. In addition, the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite displayed good separation ability, stability and reusability, which could be easily separated from the reaction system by an external magnet and reused for the next round of reaction without significant decrease of its catalytic activity. We expect that the as-prepared Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite may have the potential to replace natural peroxidase in thefields of catalysis, green chemistry, biosensors, environmental monitoring and protection.

VO2nanobelts [16], Ag2O nanoparticles [6], Cu nanoclusters [17], Au nanoparticles [18], Au@Pt nanostructures [19] and Au@PdPt alloy nanorods [20], have been investigated and found to possess intrinsic peroxidase-like activity.

Noble metal nanoparticles, such as Pt, Au and Ag nanoparticles, have attracted intensive research interest because of their unique electronic structures and excellent catalytic properties [21e23]. However, the use of noble metals is limited to some degree due to their high cost and limited supply. Compared with noble metals such as Au and Ag, Cu is a much cheap metal [24]. In the past years, much attention has been paid to prepare nanoscaled Cu, Cu2O, CuO and Cu@Cu2O composites and to investigate their application in thefield of catalysis [25,26] and environmental protection [24,25,27]. For example, Liu and co-workers fabricated porous CuO micro-/ nanostructures by a two-step route and probed their peroxidaselike activity [28]. Zhou and co-workers developed a two-step solvothermal method to prepareflower-like Cu@Cu2O nanocomposites and found that some of the Cu@Cu2O nanocomposites exhibited higher photocatalytic performance than pure Cu2O [29]. Ai and co-workers developed an interfacial hydrothermal method for the preparation of Cu@Cu2O core-shell composite microspheres and investigated their visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity [30]. Kou and co-workers synthesized porous Cu@Cu2O nanoparticles through a two-step strategy involving dealloying and

zhwangs@163.com, zhwangs@cwnu.edu.cn (Z. Wang).

surface oxidation, and investigated the photocatalytic performance and adsorption behavior of the as-prepared nanoparticles [24,25]. In order to overcome the problem of recycling Cu@Cu2O nanoparticles from the reaction system, Wang and co-workers introduced magnetic Fe3O4into the Cu@Cu2O nanoparticles and prepared magnetically recyclable Fe3O4@Cu2O@Cu nanocomposites by a two-step method [31]. However, some of the materials are synthesized through complicated and multi-step procedures, and some need toxic organic solvent, or the synthesized materials are difficult to be recycled. Therefore, it is still necessary for material researchers tofind a simple and convenient method to prepare Cu@Cu2O composite with easily recyclable properties. Furthermore, to the best of our knowledge, the peroxidase-like activity of Cu@Cu2O composite has not yet been systematically studied, although there are some publications about the peroxidase-like activity of Cu and CuO [17,28,32,33].

In this study, we reported a simple and facile one-step solvothermal method for the preparation of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite, and investigated the peroxidase-like activity of the nanocomposite with3,3 0 ,5,5 0 -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and o -phenylenediamine (OPDA) as model peroxidase substrates. It was demonstrated that the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite exhibited intrinsic peroxidase-like activity, as it could catalyze the oxidation of TMB and OPDA to produce colored products in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Additionally, the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite can be easily magnetically recycled and reused without significant loss of the catalytic activity, indicating that the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite has the potential for the replacement of natural peroxidases in practical applications.


# 2. Experimental



# 2.1. Materials


Copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4 $ 5H2O), iron chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3 $ 6H2O), sodium acetate trihydrate (NaAc $ 3H2O), ethylene glycol (EG), o -phenylenediamine (OPDA), 3,3 0 ,5,5 0 -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30wt%) were purchased from Chendu Kelong Chemical Reagent Company (Chengdu, China). All reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used as received. Ultrapure water was utilized for the preparation of all aqueous solutions.


# 2.2. Preparation of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite


The Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite was prepared by a facile one-step solvothermal method. Typically, 1mmol CuSO4 $ 5H2O (0.25g), 2mmol FeCl3 $ 6H3O (0.54g) and20mmol NaAc $ 3H2O (2.7g) were dissolved in25mL EG solution by magnetic stirring. Then the resulting solution was transferred into a50mL Teflonlined stainless autoclave and heated at200 /C14C for10h. After cooled down to room temperature naturally, the product was collected by an external magneticfield, washed with water and ethanol three times, and dried at60 /C14C.


# 2.3. Characterization


Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was taken on an FEI Inspect F50microscope. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were carried out on an FEI Tecnai G2F20fi eld-emission high-resolution transmission electron microscope. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was performed by an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (Oxford instrument X-Max). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was collected by a Rigaku Dmax/Ultima IV diffractometer with monochromatized Cu k a radiation ( l ¼ 1.5418 Å). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an XPS spectrometer (Kratos XSAM800); the binding energies were adjusted with respect to the C1s binding energy of284.8eV.


# 2.4. Peroxidase-like activity


The peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite was examined by the catalytic oxidation of OPDA and TMB in the presence of H2O2. Typically, the reaction of OPDA (or TMB) with H2O2was performed at25 /C14C in100mM phosphate buffer (pH8.0) or100mM acetate buffer (pH4.0) in the presence of0.05mg mL /C0 1Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite. Unless otherwise stated, the concentrations of OPDA and H2O2were0.5and2.0mM respectively for the oxidation of OPDA, and the concentrations of TMB and H2O2were0.2and10mM respectively for the catalytic oxidation of TMB.


# 2.5. Kinetics measurements


Kinetics measurements were performed at25 /C14C with0.05mg mL /C0 1Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite in100mM phosphate buffer (pH8.0) by varying the concentration of OPDA (0.2e1.5mM) at afixed concentration of H2O2 (2.0mM) or vice versa by varying the concentration of H2O2 (0.2e6.0mM) at afixed concentration of OPDA (1.0mM). When TMB was used as chromogenic substrate, experiments were carried out in100mM acetate buffer (pH4.0) by varying the concentration of TMB (0.025e0.3mM) at afixed concentration of H2O2 (20mM) or by varying the concentration of H2O2 (2e100mM) at afixed concentration of TMB (0.2mM). The steady-state reaction rates of OPDA and TMB oxidations were obtained by monitoring the absorbance increase at416and652nm using molar absorption coefficients of16.7 [34] and39mM /C0 1cm /C0 1 [35], respectively. The apparent kinetic parameters were determined by nonlinear least squarefitting of the absorbance data to Michaelis-Menten equation ( v ¼ Vmax $ [S]/( Km þ [S])) or Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal model (1/ v ¼ ( Km/Vmax) $ (1/[S]) þ 1/Vmax), where v is the reaction rate, Vmax is the maximum rate, [S] is the substrate concentration and Km is the Michaelis constant which represents the affinity of enzyme toward substrate, the value of Km is equal to the substrate concentration at which the reaction rate is half of the maximum reaction rate.


# 3. Results and discussion



# 3.1. Characterization of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite


Fig. 1shows the XRD pattern of the as-prepared Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O sample. The diffraction peaks at2q values of43.30, 50.43, 74.13 /C14can be ascribed to the reflections of (111), (200) and (220) planes of cubic Cu (marked with & ) with lattice constant a ¼ 3.615nm (JCPDS No. 65-9743). The peaks at2q values of30.10, 35.46, 56.98, 62.57 /C14can be ascribed to the reflections of (220), (311), (511), (440) planes of cubic Fe3O4 (marked with ( ) with lattice constant a ¼ 8.391nm (JCPDS No. 65-3107). Additionally, a weak peak at36.50 /C14 (marked with A ) can be indexed to the (111) plane of Cu2O (JCPDS No. 65-3288). The relatively lower intensity for the diffraction peaks of Cu2O may be due to the tiny amounts of Cu2O. Therefore, Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O composite sample was successfully prepared by a simple one-step solvothermal method.

The SEM images show that the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O composite are composed of uniform spherical-like morphology (Fig. 2A and B). The mean diameter of the spherical-like particles is about50nm as revealed by the TEM images (Fig. 2C and D). Fig. 2E shows a typical

Fig. 1. XRD pattern of the as-prepared sample.

which was in agreement with the (111) plane distance of Fe3O4 (0.484nm tabulated from XRD) and the (110) plane distance of cubic Cu2O (0.301nm tabulated from XRD), respectively, while the zone (II) was the superposition area of Fe3O4and Cu2O.

Elemental analysis of the as-prepared Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite was performed with an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). The results show that the Cu, Fe and O elements coexist in the nanocomposite (Fig. S1A, Supporting information). The EDS elemental mapping results further confirm that the Cu, Fe elements are uniformly distributed in the nanocomposite (Fig. S1B and C, Supporting information).

The elemental composition and chemical status of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O composite were further analyzed by means of Xray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). XPS results indicated that the composite contained Fe, Cu, O, and C (Fig. 3A). The carbon peak was due to the adventitious hydrocarbon from the XPS instrument itself. The Fe, Cu and part of O peaks were from the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O sample. Fig. 3B shows the XPS spectrum of Fe2p of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O sample, the peaks at binding energies (BE) of

Fig. 2. The SEM (A and B), TEM (C and D) and HRTEM (E and F) images of the as-prepared Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite.

HRTEM image of the composite and the enlarged view of area F in Fig. 2E is depicted in Fig. 2F. The fringe spacings of crystallites (I) and (III) in Fig. 2F were about0.485and0.293nm, respectively,

712.1and724.8eV were characteristics of Fe3 þ , while the peaks at BE of709.8and722.8eV were characteristics of Fe2 þ . The satellite signal at717.6eV is attributed to the combination of Fe2 þ and Fe3 þ ,

Fig. 3. XPS spectra of the as-prepared Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O sample: (A) survey, (B) Fe2p, (C) O1s and (D) Cu2p.

for the satellite signals of Fe2 þ and Fe3 þ are at715.5and719.0eV, respectively [36,37]. The presence of Fe2 þ and Fe3 þ in the sample further confirms the formation of Fe3O4.

The O1s core-level spectrum is shown in Fig. 3C. The peak at530.1eV is in agreement with the lattice oxygen in Cu2O [38e40] and Fe3O4 [41], the peak at531.5eV can be assigned to the presence of hydroxyl species as a result of the surface-adsorbed water on the air-exposed surface of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O composite according to previous studies [38,42e44].

The XPS spectrum of Cu2p core level region for the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite displays main peaks at933.3and953.1eV which are attributed to the binding energy of Cu2p3/2and Cu2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 3D). The broad peaks of Cu2p3/2and Cu2p1/2can be further divided into two different peaks at932.8, 934.9eV and952.6, 955.1eV, respectively. The peaks at932.8and952.6eV can be attributed to Cu/Cu2O (Cu0 /Cu þ ) [45], whereas the peaks at934.9and955.1eV can be assigned to CuO/Cu(OH)2 (Cu2 þ ) [44,46]. Cu2 þ can also be distinguished by the appearance of the shake-up satellite features at ~940.6e943.5eV [47] and ~961.9eV (denoted by arrows), which evidenced the open3d9shell of Cu(II) [46,48]. The binding energy at934.9for Cu(II) was higher than the previously reported values for CuO in the range of933.4e933.9eV [40,48]. It has been reported that when the content of CuO is low, the binding energies of Cu2 þ will shift to higher values [49]. Thus the binding energy of CuO at934.9eV in the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O samples may be due to the small content of Cu2 þ on the sample surfaces [50]. From these results it is inferred that the surface of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O sample is oxidized after being exposed to air conditions. In fact, it has been reported that tiny amounts of Cu2O on the surface could be oxidized to CuO and/or Cu(OH)2species when the surface is exposed in air with humidity [30], and the tiny surface CuO/Cu(OH)2can be detected by XPS, but not by XRD. Similar phenomenon has also been observed in our previous work that Cu2 þ signal can be detected by XPS on the surface of Cu2O, but CuO and Cu(OH)2are not detected by XRD [27]. Based on the differences between the XRD and XPS results, it can be reasoned that XPS is much more sensitive than XRD for surface detection. It has also been reported that XPS only detects signals within the upper5nm thickness on the surface, while the XRD can detect signals in micro-order thickness [30,51].


# 3.2. Peroxidase activity of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite


The peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite was examined by the catalytic oxidation of OPDA and TMBinthe presence of H2O2. OPDA and TMB have been widely used as typical chromogenic substrates for investigating the oxidaseand peroxidase-like activities of natural enzymes or mimetic enzymes [6,34,52]. As shown in the insets of Fig. 4A and B, the solutions of OPDA and TMB could react with H2O2to present yellow and blue color, respectively, within60min in the presence of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite, whereas, the solutions of OPDA and TMB were still colorless in the absence of the nanocomposite.

The absorption spectrum of OPDA oxidized by H2O2in the presence of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite displayed a characteristic absorption band at ~416nm in the visible region (Fig. 4A) due to the formation of2,3-diaminophenazine (Fig. 4C) [34], while the TMB oxidation product showed two characteristic bands at ~370and ~652nm in the UV e Vis spectrum (Fig. 4B) due to the formation of diamine and diimine as shown in Fig. 4D [52].

Fig. 4. The UV e Vis absorption spectra of (A) OPDA and (B) TMB oxidation products in the presence and absence of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite (Insets: The photographs of OPDA and TMB solutions oxidized by H2O2in the presence and absence of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite). The chemical processes of (C) OPDA and (D) TMB oxidation.

However, the solutions of OPDA and TMB showed no obvious absorption in their UV e Vis spectra in the absence of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite (Fig. 4A and B). These results suggested that the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite possesses intrinsic peroxidaselike activity and can catalyze the oxidation of peroxidase substrates OPDA and TMB in the presence of H2O2.


# 3.3. Identification of active component


Previously, it has been reported that Fe3O4nanoparticles have peroxidase-like activity, which can catalyze the oxidation of peroxidase substrates such as OPDA and TMB in the presence of H2O2 [14]. In order to identify the active component in the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite, we prepared Fe3O4and Cu@Cu2O samples (see Supporting information and Fig. S2) and measured their peroxidase-like activity. As shown in Fig. 5and Fig. S3, the activity of Cu@Cu2O, without the presence of Fe3O4, was a little bit

Fig. 5. Comparison of the peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite with Fe3O4and Cu@Cu2O for the oxidation of OPDA.

lower than that of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O. However, without the presence of Cu@Cu2O, the peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4was dramatically decreased. In addition, the activity of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O was also a little bit higher than that of the sum of Fe3O4and Cu@Cu2O. These results suggest that the peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O mainly come from Cu@Cu2O and synergistic effect may exist between Fe3O4and Cu@Cu2O composite.

It was worth of pointing out that the peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4nanoparticles reported here was much lower than that of previous report [14]. By carefully comparing the experiment conditions, we found that the size of Fe3O4nanoparticles in our experiment is about50nm, while ~30nm in the literature, and the H2O2concentration used in our experiment was2mM, which was much lower than that used in the literature (530mM). Therefore, the lower peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4nanoparticles reported here should be due to the larger particle size and the lower H2O2concentration used in our experiment.


# 3.4. Effects of pH, catalyst concentration, temperature and time of incubation


As we know, the catalytic activity of natural enzymes, such as HPR, is dependent on solution pH, catalyst concentration and temperature, etc. So we examined these factors on the peroxidaselike activity of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite. The effect of pH wasfirstly examined with the catalytic oxidation of OPDA in100mM phosphate buffer or acetate buffer. The solution pH was adjusted by addition of a small amount of HCl or NaOH solution. For the oxidation of OPDA, the reaction activity increased with increase in solution pH from4.0to8.0, and then decreased with further increase the solution pH up to10.0 (Fig. 6A). As of TMB oxidation, the highest reaction activity was observed at pH4.0 (Fig. S4). This was similar to the effects of pH on the peroxidase activity of HRP and other inorganic materials [53]. The effect of catalyst concentration was studied at thefixed concentrations of OPDA (0.5mM) and H2O2 (2mM). When Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O was varied in the range of0.02e0.05mg mL /C0 1 , the reaction rate was almost proportional to the catalyst dose, but the rate was not significantly increased when

Fig. 6. The effects of solution pH (A), catalyst dose (B), reaction of temperature (C) and time of incubation (D) on the peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite. Experiments were carried out at standard conditions as stated in the experimental section of2.4, except for the parameter investigated.

further increase in catalyst concentration up to0.075mg mL /C0 1 (Fig. 6B).

The effect of temperature on the peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite was investigated by varying temperature in the range of25e60 /C14C. As shown in Fig. 6C, the activity of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O increased with temperature below45 /C14C, while decreased with further increase in temperature. It was worth pointing out that when the oxidation reaction was carried out at60 /C14C, the color of the oxidation product began to fade after several minutes, indicating that the colored product was easily to be degraded by H2O2in the presence of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite. This might be one of the reasons that the catalytic activity was decreased at high temperature, especially higher than50 /C14C. Similar phenomena are also observed by other researchers when Fe3O4nanoparticles [14] and CuS nanoparticles [54] were used as mimetic peroxidases.

The storage of natural enzymes (proteins), especially enzyme solutions, usually requires low temperature (Usually /C0 80to4 /C14C), for natural enzymes is apt to be deteriorated at high temperature, even at room temperature conditions. To test the temperature stability of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite, we measured the peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite after incubation in water at40 /C14C. More than87% catalytic activity of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite could be retained after30h of incubation (Fig. 6D). Furthermore, the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite could still retain more than90% of its initial peroxidaselike activity within a storage period of three weeks in water at room temperature, further indicating the good temperature stability of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite as mimetic peroxidase.


# 3.5. Steady-state kinetics study


Steady-state kinetics assays were carried out to further understand the mechanism of the peroxidase-like activity of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite. The rates of product formation of OPDA and TMB oxidations were determined from the increases in the absorbances at416 [34] and652nm [35], respectively. The experiments were performed withfixed concentration of H2O2and varying the concentration of OPDA (or TMB) or vice versa in the presence of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. 7and Fig. S5, the plots of reaction rate vs. OPDA (or TMB) and H2O2concentration show typical Michaelis-Menten behavior [55]. The kinetic parameters, such as the Michaelis constant ( Km) and maximum velocity ( Vmax) were obtained by using the nonlinear least squarefitting of the data to Michaelis-Menten equation and also by using the Lineweaver-Burk plots of the double reciprocal model. Data from both models were in good agreement and were summarized in Table1and Table S1. By comparing with the kinetic parameters of other reported inorganic materials and natural peroxidase HRP (Table1and Table S1), it can be seen that the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite possesses intrinsic peroxidaselike activity and shows good affinity toward OPDA, TMB and H2O2. For example, The Km and Vmax values were0.85mM and13.1 /C2 10 /C0 8Ms /C0 1for OPDA and2.3mM and11.9 /C2 10 /C0 8Ms /C0 1for H2O2, respectively. These values are comparable to those of natural peroxidase HRP (Table1).

In addition, the change of the maximum absorbance of OPDA oxidation at416nm was linear increase with the concentration of H2O2from0.4to1.5mM with limit of detection of0.2mM (Fig. S6A). When TMB was used as chromogenic substrate, the absorbance change at652nm was linear increase with the

# 3.6. Recyclability and reusability


To test the recyclability and reusability of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite, the catalytic oxidation of OPDA in the presence of H2O2was examined. After thefirst round of experiment, the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O catalyst was separated by an external magneticfield from the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O-OPDA-H2O2reaction system and washed with water three times, then the recycled Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O was reused to catalyze the oxidation of OPDA under the same experimental conditions as thefirst round. As shown in Fig. 8A, the

Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite could be successfully recycled by a magnet. The catalytic activity was not significantly lost with three cycles (Fig. 8B).


# 4. Conclusion


In summary, we demonstrated a facile and simple one-step method for the preparation of magnetically recyclable Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite. The as-prepared Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite exhibited highly efficient peroxidase-like activity, it can catalyze the oxidation of peroxidase substrates such as OPDA and TMB in the presence of H2O2. A simple colorimetric method for H2O2detection could be established based on the peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite. Furthermore, the as-prepared Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite can be easily recycled and reused without significant loss of its catalytic activity. The

Fig. 8. (A) The schematic illustration of the magnetic recycling procedure of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite. (B) The cyclability of Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite for the catalytic oxidation of OPDA.

recyclability, reusability and nonpolluting nature of the Fe3O4@Cu@Cu2O nanocomposite would have potential applications in thefield of catalysis, biosensor, environmental monitoring and protection.

# Preparation of Fe3O4and Cu@Cu2O


Fe3O4was prepared by dissolving2mmol FeCl3·6H3O (0.54g) and20mmol NaAc·3H2O (2.7g) into25mL ethylene glycol (EG) solution under magnetic stirring. Then the resulting solution was transferred into a50mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated at200 °C for10h. After cooled to room temperature naturally, the product was collected, washed with water and ethanol three times, and dried at60 °C.

Cu@Cu2O was prepared by the same procedure as mentioned above except1mmol CuSO4·5H2O (0.25g) was added to the EG solution instead of FeCl3·6H3O.

China IP: 212.95.5.168On: Mon, 18Jun2018 09:36:11Copyright: American Scientific Publishers Delivered by Ingenta We found that the peroxidase-like activity of gold nanoparticles (GNPs) followed the MichaelisMenten kinetic model and was dependent on environmental pH and temperature, which was very similar to natural Horseradish Peroxidase (HRP). However, unlike HRP , which needs a lower H2O2concentration with a very narrow range to reach a maximum reaction rate and avoid enzyme poisoning, GNPs have very high activity, even at an H2O2concentration two orders of magnitude higher than HRP. It was demonstrated that H2O2treatment could enhance the peroxidase-like activity of GNPs, resulting thus in the activity increase in a circular catalytic reaction by the reduplicative use of GNPs. It was also found that the peroxidase-like activity of GNPs responded sensitively to nanoparticle size and surface modifications. When used in an immunoassay, GNPs were generally conjugated with antibody and blocked with hydrophilic macromolecules to construct a nanoprobe. This strongly reduced the peroxidase-like activity and detection sensitivity of GNPs, therefore, restricting their use as peroxidase mimetics. We presented a novel strategy that combined the nanoprobes with gold staining to expose fresh catalytic gold surfaces and obtained a great increase in detection sensitivity. Keywords: Gold Nanoparticles, Peroxidase-Like Activity, Gold Staining, ELISA. # 1. INTRODUCTION Natural enzymes, which possess high substrate affinity and efficiency in catalytic reactions under mild conditions, have been widely studied as catalysts. However, natural enzymes are difficult to extract and easy to deactivate in unfavourable conditions, 1 /commaori2artificial enzymes need to be developed to duplicate the advantages of natural enzymes but avoid their disadvantages and are more affordable as well. Nanostructures have attracted great attention because of their unique electrical, magnetic, optical, catalytic and other physical and chemical properties. 3-5The intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of magnetite nanoparticles (MNPs) was discovered by the Yan Group, 6which exhibits a great potential for special immunoassays when conjugating MNPs with antibody, with simultaneous capture, separation and detection capabilities. This finding ∗ Authors to whom correspondence should be addressed. provided a new idea that the nanoparticles could be used as new detection tools in biological diagnosis. Afterwards, there were more studies on nanostructured enzyme mimics as diverse as V2O5Nanowires, 7Prussian blue nanoparticles, 8Hemin-Graphene Hybrid Nanosheets, 9apoferritin-platinum nanoparticle composites (Pt-Ft) 10and Au@Pt nanorods. 11 /commaori12Gold nanoparticles, as a kind of famous functional nanomaterials, have been widely applied in biological and chemical detection due to their unique surface plasmon resonance (SPR) and surface chemical properties. They are generally modified with biocompatible molecules, such as glucose oxidase, immunoglobulin G (IgG), protein A, and human chorionic gonadotrophin to construct specific gold nanoprobes. 13-15These gold nanoprobes can be easily detected by spectrophotometry, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), electrochemical analysis, and light scattering analysis. 16Therefore, they are important tools for developing immunoassay and DNA hybridization detection methods. Because of their size and surface effects, gold nanoparticles can also be used in catalysis, especially for environmental and biological applications. Recently, GNPs were found to have intrinsic catalytic activity for H2O2decomposition and superoxide scavenging at different pHs by using electron spin resonance spectroscopy (ESR), 17meaning that GNPs might be used for new detection methods based on their enzyme-like activity. But the sensitivity of immunoassays catalyzed by nanoparticles as peroxidase mimetics or labels is limited due to their surface modifications with biomacromolecules which prevent the surface catalytic reaction. 6In other words, the active centers of nanoparticles were blocked by antibodies and other protein modifications on their surfaces. In this study, we considered gold nanoparticles as pH-dependent enzymes that could mimic peroxidases in an acid environment. Then we discovered that they could be reused as peroxidase mimetics and had peroxidase-like activity in biodetection, that is, they could be used in ELISA assays instead of HRPs. Gold staining, a procedure for electroless deposition of reduced gold irons where the GNPs act as nuclei, was established to reverse the negative effects of antibody surface modifications and increase the positivity/negativity (P/N) ratio. In this technique, the antibody conjugated gold nanoprobes act as detection labels and catalyst nuclei. They adsorb gold ions and promote their reduction into the surface of metallic gold nuclei. The process increases the amount of freshly exposed gold surface and accelerates catalysis of chromogenic substrates during biodetection. # 2. MATERIALS AND METHODS # 2.1. CONCLUSIONS We evaluated the peroxidase-like activity of GNPs. The peroxidase-like catalysis of GNPs followed MichaelisMenten kinetics and was dependent on the environmental pH, temperature, and the size and surface modifications of the GNPs. The peroxidase-like activity of GNPs could be enhanced by H2O2treatment, resulting thus in the activity increase in a circular catalytic reaction. We also designed and evaluated GNPs for ELISA applications. When used in an immunoassay, the surface of GNPs are usually covered with antibodies and other biomacromolecules, which blocks the catalytic active sites and reduces the catalysis ability of GNPs dramatically. To get fresh, exposed gold catalyst surface, we developed a gold staining method that increases the assay's sensitivity and avoids the negative effects of surface modifications. The colorimetric analysis is simple, cheap and rapid, which provides significant improvement for the immunoassay. Acknowledgment: We would like to thank Barbara Berman for editorial assistance. You are helpful assistant in chemistry, specializing in nanozymes. Your task is to analyze scientific articles and extract detailed information about various experiments with nanozymes. It is crucial for you to accurately and comprehensively describe each experiment separately, without referring to other experiments in the article.

Usually, the articles contain several experiments with nanozymes with different parameters, such as:
- Formula (e.g. Fe3O4)
- Activity (usually peroxidase, oxidase, catalase or laccase)
- Syngony (usually cubic, hexagonal, tetragonal, monoclinic, orthorhombic, trigonal, amorphous or triclinic)
- Length, width and depth (or just size or diameter)
- Surface chemistry (naked by default or poly(ethylene oxide), poly(N-Vinylpyrrolidone), Tetrakis(4-carboxyphenyl)porphine or other)
- Polymer used in synthesis (none or poly(N-Vinylpyrrolidone), oleic acid, poly(ethylene oxide), BSA or other)
- Surfactant (none or l-ascorbic acid, ethylene glycol, sodium citrate, cetrimonium bromide, citric acid, trisodium citrate, ascorbic acid or other)
- Molar mass, Michaelis constant Km, molar maximum reaction rate Vmax
- Reaction type (substrat + co-substrat) (TMB + H2O2, H2O2 + TMB, TMB, ABTS + H2O2, H2O2, OPD + H2O2, H2O2 + GSH or other)
- Minimum concentration of the substrate when measuring catalytic activity C_min (mM)
- Maximum concentration of the substrate when measuring catalytic activity C_max (mM)
- Concentration of the co-substrate when measuring the catalytic activity (mM)
- Concentration of nanoparticles in the measurement of catalytic (mg/mL)
- pH at which the catalytic activity was measured and temperature at which the research was carried out (°C).

You need to find all the experiments with different values mentioned in the article and extraction them as separate objects. It's imperative that each of these elements is addressed independently for every experiment, providing a complete and isolated description with accurate measurements in appropriate units. This approach will ensure a comprehensive and clear understanding of each experiment as an individual entity within the scientific literature on nanozymes.

CRITICAL RULES FOR EXTRACTION:
1. Keep the numerical values in the right units of measurement. It is critically important to extract all the numerical values as in the example, especially important are formula, activity, syngony, length, width, depth (size or diameter), Km, Vmax, reaction type.
2. Usually such parameters as Michaelis constant Km (mM), Vmax, mM/s are obtained in two experiments for every type of nanoparticle. You must determine what type of reaction such parameters as Michaelis constant Km (mM), Vmax, mM/s belong to.

LOGIC FOR REACTION TYPES (TRACKS):
- Reaction type is H2O2+TMB when H2O2 is a substrate and TMB in co-cubstrate.
- Reaction type is TMB +H2O2 when TMB is a substrate and H2O2 in co-cubstrate.

For example in pair H2O2 and TMB:
- In first case (you call this case as Reaction type TMB+H2O2): H2O2 plays role as a co-substrate with constant concentration(C(const), mM) and TMB plays role as a substrate with concentrations from Cmin,mM to Cmax, mM.
- In second case (you call this case as Reaction type H2O2+TMB): TMB plays role as a co-substrate with constant concentration(C(const), mM) and H2O2 plays role as a substrate with concentrations from Cmin,mM to Cmax, mM.
